detect.predict.instructions: Decide whether the supplied passage was generated by an AI.
detect.predict.signature:
StringSignature(text -> reasoning, is_ai
    instructions='Decide whether the supplied passage was generated by an AI.'
    text = Field(annotation=str required=True json_schema_extra={'desc': 'Passage to classify', '__dspy_field_type': 'input', 'prefix': 'Text:'})
    reasoning = Field(annotation=str required=True json_schema_extra={'desc': '${reasoning}', '__dspy_field_type': 'output', 'prefix': 'Reasoning:'})
    is_ai = Field(annotation=bool required=True json_schema_extra={'desc': 'True for AI-generated text; otherwise False', '__dspy_field_type': 'output', 'prefix': 'Is Ai:'})
)

fields.name: is_ai
fields.description: True for AI-generated text; otherwise False

demos.text: As an example of why this is useful, consider writing a DAG that processes a daily set of experimental data. It's been rewritten, and you want to run it on the previous 3 months of data---no problem, since Airflow can backfill the DAG and run copies of it for every day in those previous 3 months, all at once.

For a practical case, imagine you maintain a DAG that processes experimental data each day. After rewriting it, you need to apply the new version to the prior three months—no problem: Airflow can backfill the DAG, running a separate copy for every day in that period, all at once (yes, every daily interval), consistently and concurrently.

Although simpler DAGs usually fit in one Python file, more complex DAGs are often split across several files and may rely on dependencies that you need to ship alongside them (“vendored”), keeping the workflow distributed but the package complete.

In general, we advise you to try and keep the topology (the layout) of your DAG tasks relatively stable; dynamic DAGs are usually better used for dynamically loading configuration options or changing operator options.
